Maths - Number and operations.skill: Place value models - tens and ones
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Place value models - tens and ones for Grade 2 students (TEKS 2.2(A)). Students use visual and physical models to understand place value. Base-ten blocks, bundles of sticks, and place value charts help students see that digits in different positions represent different values. Key concepts include representing numbers with models, reading models to determine the number shown, and understanding the relationship between ones, tens, and hundreds. Vocabulary: place value, model, base-ten blocks, ones, tens, hundreds, represent, value, digit. Common misconceptions include miscounting blocks, confusing tens rods with ones cubes, and not connecting models to written numbers. Activities should include building numbers with blocks, matching models to numerals, and comparing numbers using models appropriate for ages 7-8.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Convert to/from a number - tens and ones
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Convert to/from a number - tens and ones for Grade 2 students (TEKS 2.2(A)). Students learn to flexibly convert between different place value representations. Key concepts include understanding that 1 ten equals 10 ones, numbers can be represented in multiple equivalent ways using different groupings, and this flexibility is essential for regrouping in addition and subtraction. Vocabulary: convert, regroup, exchange, tens, ones, hundreds, equivalent, trade. Common misconceptions include thinking there is only one way to represent a number and difficulty understanding that regrouping does not change the total value. Activities should involve trading games with base-ten blocks and solving puzzles requiring flexible place value thinking appropriate for ages 7-8.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Regroup tens and ones - ways to make a number
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Regroup tens and ones - ways to make a number for Grade 2 students (TEKS 2.2(A)). Regrouping involves exchanging equivalent amounts between place values. Students learn that numbers can be composed and decomposed in multiple ways while maintaining the same total value. For example, 35 can be 3 tens and 5 ones, or 2 tens and 15 ones. Key concepts include understanding that regrouping preserves total value and is foundational for addition with carrying and subtraction with borrowing. Vocabulary: regroup, trade, exchange, compose, decompose, equivalent, bundle. Common misconceptions include believing each number has only one representation and not understanding that regrouping maintains value. Activities should include manipulative-based trading and 'ways to make a number' exercises appropriate for ages 7-8.

Key concepts include locating and labeling numbers, understanding that numbers increase from left to right, identifying numbers between given points, and using equal intervals. Common misconceptions include not attending to scale, placing numbers without considering equal spacing, and confusion when the line doesn't start at zero. Activities should involve placing numbers on blank number lines, identifying mystery numbers, and using number lines for comparison and ordering tasks appropriate for ages 7-8.

Key concepts include using place value to decompose, adding or subtracting each part sequentially, and tracking running totals. Vocabulary: break apart, decompose, place value, partial sums, strategy. Common misconceptions include choosing unhelpful decompositions and errors in sequential operations. Activities should include step-by-step recording, base-ten block modeling, and strategy comparison appropriate for ages 7-8. When creating content, build from concrete manipulatives to pictorial representations to abstract notation, ensuring 7-8-year-olds develop both conceptual understanding and procedural fluency.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Use models to subtract a one-digit number from a two-digit number - without regrouping
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Use models to subtract a one-digit number from a two-digit number - without regrouping for Grade 2 students (TEKS 2.4(B)). Regrouping involves exchanging equivalent amounts between place values. Students learn that numbers can be composed and decomposed in multiple ways while maintaining the same total value. For example, 35 can be 3 tens and 5 ones, or 2 tens and 15 ones. Key concepts include understanding that regrouping preserves total value and is foundational for addition with carrying and subtraction with borrowing. Vocabulary: regroup, trade, exchange, compose, decompose, equivalent, bundle. Common misconceptions include believing each number has only one representation and not understanding that regrouping maintains value. Activities should include manipulative-based trading and 'ways to make a number' exercises appropriate for ages 7-8.
